eli3: Autocorrelation (serial correlation) measures the correlation between a time series and its lagged values. Positive autocorrelation means that above-average values tend to be followed by above-average values — the series is 'persistent'. Negative autocorrelation means alternating above/below average — the series 'oscillates'.

The Partial Autocorrelation Function (PACF) measures the correlation between a value and its lagged value after removing the effect of intermediate lags. The ACF and PACF patterns together identify the order of ARMA models: an AR(p) process has a PACF that cuts off after lag p; an MA(q) process has an ACF that cuts off after lag q; an ARMA process has both tailing off gradually.

Autocorrelation in regression residuals violates the OLS assumption of independent errors, causing standard errors to be wrong (typically underestimated) and t-statistics to be inflated. The Durbin-Watson test detects first-order autocorrelation in OLS residuals. Remedies: add lagged dependent variable, use Newey-West (HAC) standard errors, or explicitly model the autocorrelation structure.

whenWhy.title: Model identification in Box-Jenkins methodology
whenWhy.body: After confirming stationarity, plot the sample ACF and PACF to identify the ARMA order. The theoretical ACF/PACF patterns: AR(1): ACF decays exponentially, PACF cuts off at lag 1. MA(1): ACF cuts off at lag 1, PACF decays. ARMA(1,1): both decay. These patterns guide model selection before formal AIC/BIC comparison. The Box-Jenkins methodology: identify → estimate → diagnose → forecast.

whenWhy.title: Detecting residual misspecification
whenWhy.body: After fitting a model, residuals should be white noise (ACF approximately zero for all lags). The Ljung-Box test checks this formally: it combines the squared autocorrelations across multiple lags into a single test statistic that follows a chi-squared distribution under the null hypothesis of white noise. A significant result indicates that the model is missing some AR or MA structure. Plot residual ACF with 95% confidence bands (roughly ±1.96 divided by the square root of n) to guide diagnosis.

whenWhy.title: Correcting for autocorrelated errors in regression
whenWhy.body: In OLS with autocorrelated errors, estimators are unbiased but inefficient and standard errors are wrong. Newey-West (HAC) standard errors are consistent under arbitrary autocorrelation and heteroscedasticity — the standard correction for time series regressions. Alternatively, include the lagged dependent variable as a regressor to absorb first-order autocorrelation in the error.

deepDiveIntro: The autocovariance at lag k is the expected product of deviations from the mean at times t and t-k. The ACF is the autocovariance divided by the variance, giving values between -1 and 1. The sample ACF at lag k is estimated from the data by computing this average product over all available pairs. Under the null hypothesis that the series is white noise, each sample ACF value is approximately normally distributed with standard deviation 1 divided by the square root of n, so values outside ±1.96/√n are considered significant at the 5% level.

The PACF at lag k is the correlation between observations k steps apart after removing the linear influence of all intermediate lags. For an AR(p) process, the PACF drops to zero exactly after lag p. The PACF is estimated via Yule-Walker equations or Durbin-Levinson recursion.

deepDive.heading: ACF and PACF for ARMA Models
deepDive.body: For an AR(p) process, the theoretical ACF decays geometrically or in a sinusoidal pattern, while the PACF cuts off sharply to zero after lag p. For an MA(q) process, the ACF cuts off sharply to zero after lag q, while the PACF decays gradually. For an ARMA(p,q) process, both the ACF and PACF decay gradually without a sharp cutoff. In practice, use 95% significance bands to judge which lags are meaningful, and use AIC/BIC to select orders formally. [1]
deepDive.formula: ACF: ρ(k) = γ(k)/γ(0);   PACF: φₖₖ = Corr(Yₜ, Yₜ₋ₖ | Yₜ₋₁,…,Yₜ₋ₖ₊₁)

deepDive.heading: Ljung-Box Test for White Noise
deepDive.body: The Ljung-Box test checks whether a group of autocorrelations are all zero — that is, whether the series (or residuals) behave like white noise. It combines the squared sample autocorrelations up to lag K, weighted by a correction for finite sample size, into a single test statistic that follows a chi-squared distribution. A significant result means some autocorrelation is present and the model needs to be revised. For raw series, use K degrees of freedom; for ARMA(p,q) residuals, subtract p and q from the degrees of freedom. [1]

deepDive.heading: Newey-West HAC Standard Errors
deepDive.body: When OLS residuals are serially correlated, the standard error estimates are unreliable — they are typically too small, making t-statistics look more significant than they really are. Newey-West (HAC) standard errors correct for this by using a weighted sum of the autocovariances of the residuals, with weights that decrease for lags further apart (Bartlett kernel). The result is a sandwich-type variance estimator that is consistent under arbitrary autocorrelation and heteroscedasticity. The bandwidth (number of lags to include) is typically chosen using a rule based on the sample size. [1]

deepDive.heading: Durbin-Watson Test for AR(1) Errors
deepDive.body: The Durbin-Watson statistic tests whether consecutive OLS residuals are correlated. It is approximately equal to 2 times (1 minus the first-order autocorrelation of the residuals): a value near 2 indicates no autocorrelation, values below 2 indicate positive autocorrelation, and values above 2 indicate negative autocorrelation. The test has an inconclusive zone and only detects first-order autocorrelation. When the model includes a lagged dependent variable, the Breusch-Godfrey LM test is more appropriate since the Durbin-Watson statistic is biased toward 2 in that case. [1]

takeaways: ACF: ρ(k) = Corr(Yₜ, Yₜ₋ₖ); PACF: Corr(Yₜ,Yₜ₋ₖ | Yₜ₋₁,…,Yₜ₋ₖ₊₁). [1]
AR(p): PACF cuts off at lag p; MA(q): ACF cuts off at lag q; ARMA: both decay. [1]
Ljung-Box Q ~ χ²(K) under white noise; use K−p−q df for ARMA residuals. [1]
Newey-West HAC SEs are consistent under arbitrary autocorrelation — the standard fix for time series OLS. [1]
DW ≈ 2(1−r̂(1)): DW < 2 → positive autocorrelation; DW > 2 → negative. [1]

edgeCases.q: Why can the sample ACF be misleading for non-stationary series?
edgeCases.a: For a random walk, the theoretical ACF is approximately 1 for all lags (shocks are permanent, so the series is highly correlated with itself at any lag). The sample ACF decays slowly from 1 — this is the signature of a non-stationary (I(1)) series. When you see slowly decaying sample ACF, difference the series before interpreting ACF/PACF for model identification. Fitting ARMA to a non-stationary series produces meaningless coefficient estimates. [1]

edgeCases.q: How many lags should you test in the Ljung-Box test?
edgeCases.a: There is no universally optimal K. Box & Jenkins suggested K = min(20, n/4). For monthly data (n = 120): K = 20–30. For financial daily returns (n = 250+): K = 20. After fitting ARMA(p,q): degrees of freedom = K − p − q, so K should be much larger than p + q. For residual checking, plot the full residual ACF with ±1.96/√n bands and use K = 20–40 with the Ljung-Box test. The choice of K affects power — too large K dilutes power against alternatives with autocorrelation at specific lags. [1]

sources.label: Box, Jenkins, Reinsel & Ljung — Time Series Analysis (5th ed.), Ch. 2–3
